questions.question: What does wrapping a component in `React.memo` do?
options: It skips re-rendering the component when its props are shallow-equal to the previous render
It caches the component's return value forever and never re-renders it again
It deep-clones the props on every render to detect nested changes
It moves the component's rendering onto a background thread
questions.explanation: `React.memo` short-circuits the default behavior (children re-render when the parent does) by skipping the render when props are shallow-equal. It does not cache forever, deep-compare, or use a separate thread.

questions.question: React's shallow prop comparison uses `Object.is` on each prop. How are objects, arrays, and functions compared?
options: By reference — two distinct references are never considered equal
By structural deep equality of their contents
By JSON serialization of the value
By their `.toString()` output
questions.explanation: Primitives compare by value, but objects/arrays/functions compare by reference under `Object.is`; two distinct references never match, which is why a fresh reference each render defeats `memo`.

questions.question: Passing a new inline arrow function as a prop to a `React.memo` child on every render defeats the memoization, so the child re-renders anyway.
questions.explanation: A fresh function reference never matches the previous one under `Object.is`, so the shallow check fails and the memoized child re-renders despite identical data.

questions.question: What is the key difference between `useMemo` and `useCallback`?
options: `useMemo` caches the value returned by calling the function; `useCallback` caches the function reference itself
`useMemo` runs after render while `useCallback` runs before render
`useMemo` is for async work and `useCallback` is for synchronous work
`useCallback` re-runs on every render while `useMemo` runs only once
questions.explanation: `useMemo(() => compute(), deps)` caches the computed value; `useCallback(fn, deps)` caches the function reference and is equivalent to `useMemo(() => fn, deps)`.

questions.question: In the `List`/`Row` example, why is `onSelect` wrapped in `useCallback((id) => ..., [])`?
options: To give `onSelect` a stable identity so the memoized `Row` components can skip re-rendering
Because `console.log` cannot be called from an inline function in JSX
To memoize the result of clicking each row so clicks are not double-handled
Because `useCallback` is required whenever a function is defined inside a component
questions.explanation: Without `useCallback`, `onSelect` is a new function reference each render, defeating `memo` on every `Row`. The stable identity lets the memoized rows skip re-rendering when data is unchanged.

questions.question: When you pass a custom comparison function as `memo(Comp, (prev, next) => ...)`, what does returning `true` mean?
options: Treat the props as equal and SKIP the re-render
Treat the props as changed and force a re-render
Fall back to React's default shallow comparison
Throw away the previous render and remount the component
questions.explanation: In the custom comparator, `true` means "equal, skip the render" and `false` means "re-render" — the opposite intuition of an event handler, which is why forgetting a prop ships stale UI.

questions.question: Why is wrapping every component in `React.memo` not recommended?
options: `memo` adds a prop-comparison cost on every render, so it only pays off for components that render often with the same props or are expensive to render
`memo` permanently freezes a component so it can never receive new props again
`memo` disables React's reconciliation and breaks the virtual DOM diff
`memo` forces all child components to also be wrapped in `memo` or they error
questions.explanation: Memoization is not free: the shallow comparison runs every render. For cheap components that rarely benefit, the comparison cost and added noise outweigh the savings — measure and target the hot path.

questions.question: What is the consequence of a MISSING dependency in a `useMemo`/`useCallback` dependency array?
options: The cached function/value can read stale values via an out-of-date closure
React throws a runtime error and unmounts the component
The cache is recomputed on every single render, defeating memoization
The dependency is automatically inferred at build time, so nothing happens
questions.explanation: A missing dep means the memoized closure keeps a stale view of that value. (An over-eager/extra dep is the opposite problem — it changes too often and defeats the cache.) The `react-hooks/exhaustive-deps` lint rule enforces honest deps.

questions.question: It is safe to rely on `useMemo` for program correctness, because React guarantees the cached value is preserved across renders as long as the dependencies are unchanged.
questions.explanation: `useMemo`/`useCallback` are performance hints, not guarantees — React may discard a cached value (e.g. to free memory) and recompute. Never rely on them for correctness, only for performance.

questions.question: What does the React Compiler do, and when does it run?
options: It auto-memoizes components and the values inside them at build time, doing automatically what `memo`/`useMemo`/`useCallback` do by hand
It rewrites class components into function components at runtime
It strips all `useMemo`/`useCallback` calls and replaces them with `setTimeout` scheduling
It compiles JSX into WebAssembly for faster rendering in the browser
questions.explanation: The React Compiler is an opt-in, build-time optimizing compiler that inserts fine-grained memoization automatically, making most hand-written memoization unnecessary. It does not run at runtime or target WebAssembly.

questions.question: A component mutates a prop during render, violating the Rules of React. With the React Compiler enabled, what happens to that component?
options: The compiler skips it (leaves it un-optimized) rather than miscompiling it
The compiler silently rewrites the mutation into immutable state and optimizes it
The build fails because the compiler refuses to emit any output
The compiler memoizes it anyway, which can produce incorrect rendering
questions.explanation: The compiler can only memoize safely when code is pure. Code that breaks the Rules of React is skipped (left un-optimized) rather than miscompiled — so you lose the optimization but not correctness. The ESLint plugin flags such code so you can fix the purity violation.

questions.question: Which statements about the React Compiler are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: It reached a stable 1.0 release in 2025 and is opt-in (e.g. via `babel-plugin-react-compiler` or a build-tool integration)
It is additive — existing `useMemo`/`useCallback`/`memo` keep working alongside it
`eslint-plugin-react-hooks` ships compiler rules (in `recommended-latest`) that flag code the compiler can't optimize
Once enabled it guarantees every component in the app is fully optimized with no measurement needed
It only works on class components and ignores function components
questions.explanation: The compiler is a stable-1.0 (2025), opt-in, additive tool whose ESLint guardrail flags un-optimizable code. It does NOT guarantee universal optimization (you should still measure), and it targets function components, not classes.

questions.question: In the `Search` example, why is `setQuery(next)` called directly while `setResults(filter(next))` is wrapped in `startTransition`?
options: The input value is an urgent update that must stay responsive; the heavy list update is marked non-urgent so it renders at low priority without blocking typing
`startTransition` is required for any function that calls another function
`setQuery` cannot be wrapped in `startTransition` because it updates a string
Wrapping `setQuery` would make the input read-only until the list finishes
questions.explanation: Updates inside `startTransition` are marked non-urgent (low priority). Keeping `setQuery` urgent keeps the controlled input responsive, while the expensive `setResults` renders in the background.

questions.question: What is the `isPending` boolean from `useTransition` used for?
options: To show a subtle pending indicator while the low-priority transition render is in flight, without blocking input
To prevent the user from typing until the transition completes
To indicate that a network request triggered by the transition has failed
To force the transition to run synchronously and finish immediately
questions.explanation: `isPending` is true while the non-urgent render is in flight, letting you display a spinner/dimmed state without freezing the urgent input. It does not block input or relate to network errors.

questions.question: You receive a `query` only as a prop from a parent — you do NOT own the state setter. Which API lets you make the expensive derived render trail behind without freezing the UI?
options: `useDeferredValue(query)`
`useTransition()` wrapping the parent's setter
`React.memo` with a custom comparator on `query`
`useCallback` keyed on `query`
questions.explanation: Use `useTransition` when you own the state setter (you can wrap the `set` call); use `useDeferredValue` when you only hold the value (e.g. a prop) and want it to lag behind. Here you only have the prop, so `useDeferredValue` is correct.

questions.question: On the first render where `query` changes to a brand-new value, what does `useDeferredValue(query)` return, and what is the `query !== deferredQuery` idiom for?
questions.code:
const deferredQuery = useDeferredValue(query);
const isStale = query !== deferredQuery;
options: It returns the PREVIOUS value and schedules a low-priority re-render to catch up; `query !== deferredQuery` means results are stale, so show a dimmed/pending state
It returns the NEW value immediately; `query !== deferredQuery` can never be true
It returns `undefined` until the deferred render finishes; the comparison guards against that
It returns a debounced value after a fixed delay; the comparison checks if the timer is still running
questions.explanation: On a new value, `useDeferredValue` returns the previous value and schedules a low-priority re-render to catch up. While they differ, a fresh render is pending — the `query !== deferredQuery` check is the idiom for showing a stale/dimmed UI. It is not a timer-based debounce.

questions.subtopic: why deferred subtree must be memoized
questions.question: In the `useDeferredValue` example, the heavy `expensiveFilter(deferredQuery)` is wrapped in `useMemo([deferredQuery])`. Why is this memoization essential rather than optional?
options: Deferring only helps if the heavy subtree can skip re-rendering until `deferredQuery` catches up; without memoization the expensive work runs on every render and the deferral buys nothing
`useDeferredValue` throws unless its consumer is wrapped in `useMemo`
`useMemo` is what makes `deferredQuery` actually lag behind `query`
Without `useMemo`, `deferredQuery` would equal `query` and the dimming would never trigger
questions.explanation: Concurrent APIs reschedule work, they don't make it faster. If the expensive subtree re-runs on every render anyway, deferral provides no benefit — memoization (or the Compiler) is what lets the subtree skip redundant work until the deferred value catches up.

questions.question: Which statements about `useTransition`/`useDeferredValue` and concurrent rendering are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: A low-priority render can be paused, aborted, and restarted if a more urgent update arrives, throwing away stale in-progress work
They reschedule expensive work by priority but do not make the work itself faster
Pairing them with `memo`/`useMemo` (or the Compiler) helps avoid redoing work in the deferred subtree
They run the expensive render on a separate OS thread so the main thread is never touched
They convert the expensive computation into an asynchronous network call automatically
questions.explanation: Concurrent rendering is interruptible (pause/abort/restart) and reprioritizes rather than accelerates work, so it pairs with memoization. It does NOT use a separate OS thread, nor does it turn computation into a network request.
